desc: Slender, pale green-brown assassin bug. Sticky forelegs trap prey. Important garden predator.

fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; sticky forelegs; captures flies, aphids

desc: Large, dark assassin bug attracted to lights. Painful bite — one of the most painful insect bites in SoCal. Not a kissing bug.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flies to lights; painful defensive bite
fm.Habitat: Under bark, debris; enters homes at night

desc: North America's largest assassin bug. Distinctive cogwheel crest on thorax. Powerful predator. At western range edge in LA.

fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; slow; painful bite if handled
fm.Diet: Predator — caterpillars, beetles, other large insects

desc: Bloodsucking assassin bug. Vector of Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi). Bites sleeping humans at night. Found in woodrat nests.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with orange-red lateral margins
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal blood feeder; bites face/lips of sleeping hosts; defecates near bite (disease transmission)
fm.Habitat: Woodrat nests, rock crevices; enters homes

desc: Spectacular orange-and-black stink bug. Major pest of Brassicaceae. Bright aposematic warning coloration.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious; sucks plant juices; warning coloration; bad smell
fm.Habitat: Gardens, crops — wherever cruciferous plants grow

desc: Invasive from Asia. Shield-shaped brown with marbled pattern. Major agricultural pest. Aggregates in homes in fall.

fm.Behavior: Invasive; aggregates in buildings fall/winter; agricultural pest

desc: Bright green shield bug. Common on vegetables and fruit. Strong defensive odor.

fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; strong defensive smell; damages fruit

desc: Western green stink bug with pale lateral margins. Extremely common on native plants.

fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; aggregates on seed heads

desc: Large plant bug with flattened hind tibiae ('leaf feet'). White zigzag band across wings. Major citrus/pomegranate pest.

fm.Color: Dark brown; white zigzag band on wings; flattened hind legs
fm.Behavior: Plant piercer; damages fruit; gregarious nymphs; strong flier
fm.Diet: Fruit and seeds — citrus, pomegranate, tomato

desc: Western leaffooted bug — brown coreid with expanded hind tibiae. Feeds on developing seeds and fruit. Common on almonds, pistachios, tomatoes. Aggregates in fall.
Large, dark leaf-footed bug on agave and cactus. Pronounced hind femoral spines.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; robust; spiny hind femora
fm.Behavior: On agave flower stalks; slow; strong flier

desc: Dark with vivid orange border. Gregarious nymphs are metallic blue. Striking at all life stages.

fm.Color: Adults: dark with orange margins. Nymphs: metallic blue (striking)
fm.Behavior: Gregarious; feeds on many plants; nymphs conspicuously blue

desc: Red-orange and black seed bug on milkweed. Sequesters cardiac glycosides.

fm.Color: Red-orange and black; X-pattern on wings
fm.Behavior: Gregarious on milkweed; toxic; warning coloration

desc: Larger milkweed bug. Orange bands on black. Classic aposematic insect used in biology research.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious on milkweed seed pods; toxic

desc: Black with red/orange lines. Aggregates en masse on sun-warmed walls in fall. Nuisance pest in homes.

fm.Behavior: Mass aggregations on warm walls; enters homes fall/winter

desc: Invasive from Africa (arrived SoCal 2008). Small, black-white-orange. Devastating pest of Brassicaceae crops.

fm.Color: Black with white/orange markings; shield-shaped; small
fm.Behavior: Invasive; pierces plant tissue causing severe damage; spreading rapidly

desc: Walks on water surface using hydrophobic leg hairs. Detects prey vibrations through water. Common on any slow stream or pond.

fm.Behavior: Skates on water surface film; detects vibrations; predator
fm.Habitat: Streams, ponds, any calm water surface

desc: Bizarre treehopper with tall, laterally compressed thorn-like pronotum. Tropical species established in urban SoCal.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious on ornamental trees; females guard eggs

desc: Brilliant turquoise-blue with orange spots. One of the most photogenic insects in LA County. On coast live oak.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious on oak twigs; maternal care of eggs

desc: Large leafhopper. Vector of Pierce's disease — devastating to grapevines. Invasive from SE USA. Dark with clear wings.

fm.Behavior: Powerful flier; xylem feeder; excretes copious liquid; disease vector
fm.Diet: Xylem sap — polyphagous; vectors Xylella fastidiosa

desc: Vivid blue-green and red-orange striped leafhopper. Also vectors Pierce's disease but native species.

fm.Behavior: Xylem feeder; vectors Pierce's disease; on riparian vegetation

desc: Spectacular: red and blue-green parallel stripes. Common on ornamental plants. Harmless.

fm.Color: Bright red and blue-green parallel stripes
fm.Behavior: Hops readily when disturbed; common on ornamental shrubs

desc: LA's most common summer cicada. Males produce deafening buzz from tree canopies on hot days. Desert-adapted.

fm.Sound: Loud continuous buzz on hot days (>95°F); can reach 90dB
fm.Behavior: Males sing from canopy; nymphs underground 2–5 years
fm.Habitat: Desert edge, urban trees — hot dry areas

desc: Small, dark cicada of mountain chaparral. Males produce clicks (not buzzes). Often unnoticed.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; small for a cicada
fm.Behavior: Males click from branches; cryptic

desc: Medium-sized dark cicada of foothill and mountain areas. Buzzy song.

fm.Behavior: Canopy singer; nymphs underground for years

desc: Mottled brown stink bug. Common agricultural pest. One of the most frequently encountered shield bugs.

fm.Color: Mottled brown with pale speckling; shield-shaped
fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; damages fruit; aggregates overwinter

desc: Small, variable-colored plant bug. One of the most economically important agricultural pests in western NA.

fm.Color: Variable — green, brown, reddish; V-mark on scutellum
fm.Behavior: Fast-moving; feeds on flower buds and developing fruit
fm.Diet: Flower buds, developing seeds — major crop pest

desc: Dark assassin bug with red-orange abdomen. Ambushes bees and other pollinators on flowers. Sticky resinous forelegs.

fm.Behavior: Ambushes bees on flowers; sticky resinous forelegs
fm.Diet: Predator — bees, flies, other flower visitors

desc: Second kissing bug species in LA County. Reddish-brown with orange lateral margins. Desert edge.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal blood feeder; Chagas disease vector

desc: Major pest of cucurbits. Dark, shield-shaped. Eggs copper-colored on leaf undersides. Difficult to control.

fm.Behavior: Sucks plant juices; causes wilting; overwinters under debris
fm.Habitat: Gardens — wherever squash, pumpkin, cucumber grow

desc: Red-eyed soapberry bug. Gregarious on golden rain tree seeds. Conspicuous red-and-black aggregations.

fm.Color: Black with red eyes and red shoulders/margins
fm.Behavior: Mass aggregations on fallen seeds; harmless; conspicuous
fm.Diet: Seeds of Sapindaceae — golden rain tree, balloon vine

desc: Large leaf-footed bug of desert creosote scrub. Moon-shaped hind tibial expansions.

fm.Color: Dark brown; crescent-shaped hind tibial lobes
fm.Behavior: On creosote bush; slow-moving

desc: Delicate green insect with golden eyes and lacy wings. Larvae ('aphid lions') are voracious predators. Commercially sold as biocontrol.

fm.Size: ½ in body; wings to 1 in
fm.Color: Bright green; golden eyes; delicate lacy wings
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to lights; larvae are 'aphid lions' — voracious predators

desc: Small green stink bug. Common on many plants. Often confused with other green shield bugs.

fm.Color: Green with narrow pale margins; shield-shaped
fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; common on tree foliage

desc: Predatory stink bug — one of the few. Prominent shoulder spines. Commercially used for biocontrol of pest caterpillars.

fm.Color: Brown with dark speckles; prominent shoulder spines
fm.Behavior: Predator — impales caterpillars and beetle larvae with beak

desc: Bizarre toad-shaped bug of stream margins. Bumpy texture and bulging eyes mimic a toad. Jumps to catch prey.

fm.Color: Mottled gray-brown; bumpy; toad-like; bulging eyes
fm.Behavior: Jumps like a toad to catch prey; sits on mud/sand near water
fm.Habitat: Stream margins, pond edges — on wet sand/mud

desc: Dark brown stink bug with orange/red border. Agricultural pest of beans and tomatoes.

fm.Shape: Dark brown stink bug with orange/red border

desc: Tiny predator — eats thrips, mites, aphid eggs. Important biocontrol agent. Can bite humans.

fm.Shape: Tiny predator — eats thrips, mites, aphid eggs

desc: Delicate long-legged bug on vegetation. Predatory — feeds on small insects and eggs.
Large aquatic predator to 2.5 in. Ambushes fish and frogs. Painful bite. 'Toe-biter.' Attracted to lights.
Skates on water surface film using hydrophobic leg hairs. Detects ripples from trapped insects.

fm.Shape: Skates on water surface film using hydrophobic leg hairs

desc: Pale green predatory bug lurking on flowers. Ambushes pollinators and other insects.

fm.Shape: Pale green predatory bug lurking on flowers

desc: Bordered plant bug — large dark bug with orange-red border. Nymphs brilliant metallic blue. Gregarious on various plants. One of the most photogenic true bugs.

fm.Color: Dark with orange-red margins; nymphs metallic blue

desc: Large brown bug with flattened hind legs. Feeds on conifer seeds. Enters homes in fall. Buzzy flier.

fm.Shape: Large brown bug with flattened hind legs

desc: Brown bug feeding on prickly pear pads. Causes yellowing and scarring of cactus tissue.

fm.Shape: Brown bug feeding on prickly pear pads

desc: Stick-like aquatic bug with long breathing tube. Ambush predator of tadpoles and small fish.

fm.Habitat: Stick-like aquatic bug with long breathing tube

desc: Small plant bug causing leaf stippling on ash trees. Can defoliate ornamentals in bad years.

fm.Shape: Small plant bug causing leaf stippling on ash trees

desc: European seed bug recently established in SoCal. On sycamore and boxelder seeds.
Bark-colored stink bug perfectly camouflaged on tree trunks. Feeds on plant juices.

fm.Color: Bark-brown — excellent camouflage on tree trunks

desc: Small aquatic bug swimming with oar-like legs. Herbivorous — feeds on algae. Important fish food.

fm.Habitat: Small aquatic bug swimming with oar-like legs

desc: Swims upside down using long hind legs as oars. Predatory — bites painfully if handled.

fm.Shape: Swims upside down using long hind legs as oars

desc: Extremely flat bug living under bark. Feeds on fungal hyphae in dead wood. Bark-matching camouflage.

fm.Shape: Extremely flat bug living under bark

desc: Tiny cryptic bug ambushing insects much larger than itself on flowers. Powerful raptorial forelegs.

fm.Behavior: Catches insects much larger than itself

desc: Small brown neuropteran (not a true bug). Larvae and adults eat aphids. Important biocontrol.

fm.Shape: Small brown neuropteran (not a true bug)

desc: Small dark cicada of mountain forests. Males sing from treetops. Multi-year underground larval stage.

fm.Habitat: Small dark cicada of mountain forests

desc: Tiny bug with ornate lacy wings. Feeds on sycamore leaf undersides causing stippling damage.

fm.Behavior: Causes stippling damage; can defoliate

desc: Obligate blood-feeder on humans. Flattened body hides in mattress seams. Major resurgence since 2000s.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal blood-feeder; resurgent since 2000s

desc: Predatory stink bug with red and black coloring. Preys on Colorado potato beetle larvae. Beneficial.

fm.Shape: Predatory stink bug with red and black coloring

desc: Tiny white-winged sap-feeder. Major crop pest — vectors many plant viruses. Enormous populations on tomatoes.
White wax-coated scale insect on citrus and ornamentals. Excretes honeydew promoting sooty mold.
Circular armored scale on citrus bark and fruit. One of most important citrus pests worldwide.
Largest aphid in NA — ¼ in. On oak, sycamore, pecan bark. Ant-tended. Impressive defensive leg-kicking.

fm.Size: ⅛ in–¼ in — largest NA aphid

desc: Bright yellow aphid on milkweed and oleander. Sequesters cardiac glycosides — toxic to predators. Cosmopolitan.
Soft brown scale on citrus. Major pest — honeydew promotes sooty mold. Important target for biocontrol.
White cottony mass on Opuntia cactus. Source of carmine red dye. Historically important trade commodity.

fm.Color: Dark red body under white cochineal wax

desc: Small green aphid — most important plant virus vector worldwide. On peach (primary) and many crops (secondary).
White cottony scale on citrus. Australian invasive that triggered first classical biocontrol success (Vedalia beetle 1889).

fm.Size: ⅛ in–⅓ in with white egg sac
fm.Color: Orange-brown body + white fluted egg mass

desc: Tiny mottled brown psyllid. Vectors citrus greening disease (HLB) — most serious citrus disease globally.
Tiny yellow thrips scarring citrus fruit rind. Important pest causing cosmetic damage to fruit.
Tiny white-winged plant pest. Clouds fly up when plants disturbed. Greenhouse and garden pest worldwide.
Small brown insect in herds on tree bark. Not a true louse — feeds on lichens, algae. Harmless.

fm.Habitat: Tree bark — herds visible on trunks

desc: Eastern boxelder bug. Black with red markings. Aggregates on warm walls. Occasionally reaches SoCal.
Small dark cicada of inland valleys. Males sing from shrubs. Multi-year underground development.
Delicate green insect with golden eyes (technically Neuroptera). Larvae voracious aphid lions. Sold as biocontrol.
Adult is lacewing-like insect. Larva digs sand pit traps to catch ants. Desert and sandy areas.
Tiny slender insect (technically Thysanoptera). Major crop pest and disease vector worldwide.
Small pale leafhopper — sole vector of curly top virus, devastating to tomatoes and beets. Migrates from desert to cultivated areas in spring.

fm.Disease: Sole vector of curly top virus

desc: Invasive from Asia — not yet established in CA but under active quarantine. Adults spectacular: gray forewings with black spots, red hindwings. Report sightings to CDFA.

fm.Color: Gray FW with black spots; red HW
fm.Status: QUARANTINE PEST — report sightings to CDFA

desc: Green thorn-shaped treehopper — diagnostic triangular pronotum. Feeds on plant sap. Female cuts crescent-shaped egg slits in bark. Common on alfalfa, weeds, and ornamentals.
Invasive Australian psyllid devastating eucalyptus throughout LA since 1998. Nymphs build conspicuous white crystalline 'lerps' (sugar shelters) on leaves. Can defoliate trees.

fm.Habitat: Eucalyptus — white crystalline lerps on leaves diagnostic
fm.Damage: Defoliates eucalyptus; honeydew and sooty mold

desc: Slender tan predatory bug. Important biological control agent — feeds on aphids, leafhoppers, caterpillars, and mites in agricultural fields. Resembles a thin assassin bug.

fm.Role: Biological control — feeds on aphids, leafhoppers, caterpillars

desc: Invasive Mexican whitefly — produces spectacular long white waxy filaments hanging from leaf undersides. Common on hibiscus, banana, mulberry. Arrived in LA ~1992.

fm.Size: ⅛ in body; filaments up to 2 in
fm.Color: White; produces long waxy filaments hanging from leaves
fm.Habitat: Hibiscus, banana, mulberry — conspicuous white filaments

desc: Bright yellow-green with 4 black stripes. Feeds on over 250 plant species. Causes distinctive round necrotic spots on leaves. Nymph is bright red.

fm.Damage: Round necrotic leaf spots at feeding sites

desc: Bright green sharpshooter with pointed head. Vectors Pierce's disease of grapevines (Xylella fastidiosa). Common in irrigated areas, lawns, and riparian zones.

fm.Habitat: Irrigated lawns, riparian — common vector of Xylella

desc: Small waxy-white planthopper. Nymphs produce conspicuous white waxy filaments. Polyphagous on citrus, ornamentals. Introduced from eastern US.

fm.Habitat: Citrus, ornamentals — nymphs produce white wax

desc: Shiny black aphid on citrus new growth. Vectors Citrus Tristeza Virus. Dense colonies on tender shoots cause leaf curling. Managed by parasitoid wasps and lady beetles.
Small brown froghopper — nymphs produce distinctive white frothy 'spittle' masses on plant stems. The familiar foamy blobs on garden plants. Adults jump powerfully.

fm.Color: Variable — tan, brown, or black; mottled
fm.Habitat: Garden plants — nymphal spittle masses on stems diagnostic
fm.Behavior: Nymphs produce protective foam; adults jump far

desc: One of the largest true bugs in North America — up to 1.5 in. Beautiful: dark with orange-red margins. Nymphs are brilliant metallic blue-green. Desert species on mesquite.

fm.Color: Dark with orange-red margins; nymphs metallic blue-green

desc: Extremely slender stick-like assassin bug. Steals prey from spider webs. Among the most bizarre insects.
Colonial aphid producing white woolly wax. Dense colonies on apple, pyracantha, elm. Cottony masses diagnostic.
Bright orange-red assassin bug. Sticky legs trap prey. Important garden predator. Common on milkweed and throughout gardens.
Tiny bug with intricate lace-like wings. Feeds on undersides of sycamore and alder leaves. Beautiful under magnification.
Small green-yellow aphid on cotton, cucurbits, and ornamentals. Vectors many plant viruses. One of the most polyphagous aphids.
Large green or pink aphid on roses. Very common garden pest. Dense colonies on buds and new growth. Managed by ladybugs and lacewings.
Small pale green planthopper. Sits motionless on stems. Wedge-shaped. Common on many ornamental plants.
Small dark treehopper with pronotal horn. Lives in species complex — different host-plant races. Vibrational communication through stems.

fm.Color: Dark with pale markings; pronotal horn

desc: Mediterranean red bug — striking red-and-black seed bug recently established in SoCal (~2009). Forms dense aggregations on malvaceous plants. Feeds on seeds. Expanding range.
Southern green stink bug — bright green shield bug. One of the world's most important crop pests. Strong defensive odor. Feeds on over 30 crop families. Recently arrived in LA.
Torpedo bug — bright green planthopper with pointed head. Introduced from Australia. Wedge-shaped, flattened. Jumps powerfully. Common on ornamentals and citrus.
Notched giant water bug — large aquatic predator. Males carry eggs on back (paternal care — rare in insects). Catches fish, tadpoles, aquatic insects. Painful bite if handled.

fm.Behavior: ♂ carries eggs on back — paternal care

desc: Broken-backed bug — common green plant bug. Named for the broken crossband on pronotum. Feeds on many plants. Very common in gardens and agricultural fields.

fm.Color: Green with broken pale band on pronotum

desc: California bee assassin — large red-and-black assassin bug specializing on bees. Ambushes pollinators on flowers using sticky resin on forelegs. Native and ecologically important.

fm.Behavior: Ambushes bees on flowers with sticky legs

desc: Potato mirid — green plant bug with dark punctures on pronotum. Introduced from Europe. Common pest of potatoes, legumes, and many garden plants.
Hyaline grass bug — small pale rhopalid bug on grasses and weedy plants. Very common in disturbed habitats. Congregates on drying weedy vegetation in summer.
Cabbage aphid — gray-green waxy aphid forming dense colonies on brassica crops. Covered in powdery grayish wax (unlike green peach aphid). Major cole crop pest.

fm.Habitat: Cabbage, broccoli, kale — dense waxy colonies

desc: Crown whitefly — tiny moth-like insect on undersides of oak leaves. Nymphs produce distinctive crown-shaped waxy fringe. Common on coast live oak.

fm.Color: White; moth-like; crown-shaped nymphal wax on oak

desc: Four-spined assassin bug — pale green assassin bug with four pronotal spines. Important predator of pest insects in gardens and agricultural fields. Can bite if handled.
Cluster stink bug — introduced African stink bug recently established in SoCal (~2013). Yellow-orange with black punctures. Aggregates on various plants. Expanding range.
Spring fishfly — large dark neuropteran (dobsonfly relative). Larvae aquatic in streams. Adults attracted to lights near water. Long filiform antennae (vs. pectinate in Corydalus).
Sage leafhopper — tiny cicadellid feeding on sage, rosemary, and other Lamiaceae. Causes white stippling damage. Introduced from Mediterranean. Pest of herb gardens.

fm.Damage: White stippling on sage and rosemary leaves

desc: Manzanita gall aphid — tiny aphid inducing distinctive red-purple leaf-fold galls on manzanita (Arctostaphylos). Very common in chaparral. Galls are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark; galls are red-purple leaf folds on manzanita

desc: Milkweed seed bug — small dark seed bug on milkweed. Similar to large milkweed bug but smaller and darker. Feeds on milkweed seeds in pods.
Brilliant plant bug — small attractive mirid with metallic blue-green sheen. Predatory — feeds on mites and small insects on plants. Beneficial.
Expanded plant bug — small pale mirid on various plants. Common in gardens and weedy areas. Part of the very diverse plant bug family (Miridae).
Peppertree psyllid — tiny psyllid specialist on California pepper tree (Schinus molle). Forms pit galls on leaves. Very common wherever pepper trees grow.

fm.Color: Tiny; pit galls on pepper tree leaves

desc: Whitecrossed seed bug — dark seed bug with white X-pattern on back. Common on ragwort and other Asteraceae. Conspicuous aggregations on host plants.

fm.Color: Dark with white cross on back

desc: Pacific ambush bug — tiny cryptic predator hiding in flower heads. Raptorial forelegs grab prey many times its size — catches butterflies, bees, even wasps.

fm.Behavior: Catches prey much larger than itself

desc: Thirteen-spotted leafhopper — small green leafhopper with distinctive spotted pattern. On various trees and shrubs. One of the more recognizable leafhoppers.
Adventive psyllid — introduced psyllid on various ornamental plants. Forms galls on new growth. Part of the growing guild of invasive psyllids in SoCal.

fm.Color: Pale green; galls on new growth

desc: Two-spotted leafhopper — tiny green leafhopper with two dark spots. Introduced from Asia. Pest of many ornamental plants. Vectors phytoplasma diseases.

fm.Color: Pale green with two dark spots

desc: Fly speck bug — tiny dark true bug found on bark and lichen. Named for resembling a fly speck. Easily overlooked. Bark-dwelling.

fm.Color: Dark; resembles a fly speck on bark

desc: Saturn seed bug — distinctive seed bug with pale ring markings. On various plants. Mediterranean introduction. Spreading in SoCal.
Spotted gum lerp psyllid — tiny psyllid on eucalyptus producing white crystalline 'lerps' (waxy covers). Introduced from Australia. Very common on eucalyptus.

fm.Color: Tiny; white crystalline lerps on eucalyptus

desc: Dark plant bug — small dark mirid on various plants. Very common but overlooked. Part of the hyperdiverse plant bug family.
Privet leafhopper — small green leafhopper on privet and olive. Vectors phytoplasma diseases. Very common on ornamental privet hedges.
Cotton seed bug — small dark bug with translucent wings. Introduced from Old World. Pest of cotton and malvaceous plants. Recently established in SoCal.
Spiny-collared assassin bug — orange-brown assassin bug with spiny pronotum. Predatory on garden insects. Common on vegetation. Can bite if handled.
Four-spotted leafhopper — small green leafhopper with four dark spots on wings. On various plants in gardens and chaparral.

fm.Color: Green with four dark wing spots

desc: Smoke tree sharpshooter — large leafhopper with mottled wings. Native. Similar to glassy-winged sharpshooter. On smoke tree and other desert plants.

fm.Color: Mottled brown; large for a leafhopper

desc: Margined plant bug — small mirid with dark margins. On various plants in chaparral and gardens. Plant sap feeder.
Red assassin bug — striking red-and-black assassin bug. Predatory — catches caterpillars and other insects. Common in chaparral. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
Consort leafhopper — small green-yellow leafhopper on oaks and other trees. Common in woodland habitats.
Corn leaf aphid — small dark aphid colonizing corn and grain leaves. Can build enormous colonies. Important crop pest. Honeydew attracts sooty mold.

fm.Color: Dark blue-green; colonial on corn/grain leaves

desc: Black-striped planthopper — small green planthopper with dark stripes. On grasses and sedges. Jumps powerfully when disturbed.
Pyriform scale — small pear-shaped soft scale on various ornamentals. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold. Can damage landscape plants.
Clear-winged bark louse — small psocid on tree bark and dead branches. Feeds on fungi and algae. Forms groups. Harmless.
Fire spittlebug — small black froghopper with red-orange markings. Nymphs produce frothy spittle on grass stems. Named for fire-like coloring. Native.
California pepper tree psyllid — tiny psyllid inducing pit galls on California pepper tree (Schinus molle) leaves. Very common wherever pepper trees grow in LA.

fm.Color: Tiny; pit galls on pepper tree leaves

desc: Cotton fleahopper — small pale green mirid. Pest of cotton — feeds on developing flower buds causing square loss. Common on various weedy plants.
Cactus coreid — leaf-footed bug specializing on prickly pear cactus fruit. Flattened hind tibiae. Common wherever Opuntia grows. Native.
Potato aphid — large green or pink aphid on potatoes, tomatoes, and many plants. Long cornicles. Vectors many plant viruses. Very common garden pest.

fm.Color: Green or pink; long dark cornicles

desc: Tip-marked coreid — brown leaf-footed bug on various plants. Expanded hind tibiae. Common on prickly pear and other plants in foothill habitats.
Western sycamore lace bug — tiny bug with intricate lace-patterned wings. Feeds on sycamore leaf undersides. Causes stippling damage. Beautiful under magnification.
Two-spined leafhopper — green sharpshooter with two spiny projections on vertex. Vectors Xylella. Common on oaks and riparian plants.
Red-banded sharpshooter — colorful leafhopper with red and green banding. Common on ornamentals. One of several Graphocephala sharpshooters in LA.
Willow leafhopper — green sharpshooter found on willows along riparian corridors. Similar to other Graphocephala but host-specific.
Charcoal seed bug — dark gray seed bug common in weedy areas. Aggregates in large numbers. Feeds on seeds of various plants. Often found under debris.
Uhler's stink bug — green shield bug similar to green stink bug but with white-tipped spine on each shoulder. Common on various plants. Defensive stink odor.
Van Duzee's cicada — medium-dark cicada of oak woodland. Males produce loud buzzing song. Common in foothill canyons. Named for hemipterist E.P. Van Duzee.

fm.Color: Dark; loud buzzing song in oaks

desc: Versute sharpshooter — medium green leafhopper. Vectors Pierce's disease of grapevines. Common on many ornamental and native plants.
Long-tailed mealybug — soft white waxy insect with very long waxy tail filaments (longer than body). Pest of citrus, grape, and ornamentals. Ants tend it for honeydew.

fm.Color: White waxy; tail filaments longer than body

desc: Barnacle scale — waxy scale insect resembling a small barnacle. On many ornamental and citrus plants. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold.
Mute stilt bug — extremely slender thread-like bug. Predator of mites and small insects on vegetation. So thin it's nearly invisible in profile.
F-winged barklouse — small psocid on tree bark with distinctive F-shaped wing markings. Feeds on algae and lichens. Very common on tree trunks. Gregarious.

fm.Color: Pale with F-pattern wings; on tree bark

desc: Lupine aphid — large green aphid forming colonies on lupine stems and flower buds. Long cornicles. Can damage ornamental and native lupines.

fm.Color: Green; long black cornicles; on lupine

desc: Catalina cicada — cicada associated with Santa Catalina Island and mainland coastal scrub. Males sing from shrubs. Part of California's diverse cicada fauna.
Westphalian booklouse — tiny wingless psocid found indoors in damp areas. Feeds on mold and starchy materials. Can infest old books and papers.

fm.Color: Pale; wingless; in damp indoor areas

desc: California plant bug — small green mirid on phacelia and other wildflowers. Native. Part of the enormous and diverse plant bug family.
Western big-eyed bug — tiny predatory bug with enormous eyes. Important biocontrol agent — feeds on aphids, whiteflies, mites, and insect eggs in crops.
Woolly whitefly — tiny moth-like insect producing dense woolly wax on citrus leaf undersides. Introduced from South America. Managed by parasitoid wasps.

fm.Color: White; woolly waxy tufts on citrus

desc: Bronze bug — tiny flat bug from Australia damaging eucalyptus. Causes leaf bronzing and defoliation. Invasive pest spreading through California since ~2016.
Sycamore seed bug — pale seed bug specializing on sycamore seeds. Can invade homes in large numbers in fall when sycamores drop seeds. Harmless but alarming in quantity.

fm.Behavior: Mass home invasions in fall

desc: Spiny assassin bug — dark assassin bug with spiny body surface. Predator of other insects. Found in leaf litter and under debris. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
Bluegum psyllid — tiny psyllid producing copious white waxy secretion on blue gum eucalyptus. Introduced from Australia. Can defoliate young trees.

fm.Color: Tiny; white wax on eucalyptus shoots

desc: Whitemarked fleahopper — tiny pale mirid. Major pest of cotton — damages developing squares. Also on many weedy plants. Jumps actively.
Stanford's whitefly — tiny whitefly on undersides of native tree leaves. Named for Stanford University collections. Produces waxy secretion.
Suckfly — small pale green plant bug feeding on tobacco, tomato, and other Solanaceae. Common garden pest. Causes stippling damage to leaves.
Red-winged seed bug — dark seed bug with reddish wing membrane. Under bark and in leaf litter. Feeds on seeds. Native.
Tomato bug — small green-brown mirid found on tomato plants. Both pest (feeds on plant) and predator (eats whitefly eggs). Complex ecological role.
Yerba santa plant bug — small green mirid associated with yerba santa (Eriodictyon) in chaparral. Host-specific. Part of diverse California native mirid fauna.
Morrill lace bug — tiny bug with intricate lace-patterned wings on various shrubs. Causes stippling damage to leaves. Beautiful under magnification.
Virgula plant bug — small predatory mirid feeding on mites, thrips, and small insects. Beneficial in gardens. Originally European.
Minute pirate bug — tiny but fierce predator. Important biocontrol of thrips in strawberry and other crops. Can deliver surprisingly painful bite relative to tiny size.
Armored leafhopper — small brown leafhopper with pale crossband. Common on various shrubs in chaparral and gardens.
California barklouse — small psocid on tree bark. Feeds on algae, lichen, and fungi. Gregarious — often in groups. Harmless. Common on native oaks.
Acacia psyllid — tiny psyllid on Acacia and Albizia. Introduced from Australia. Forms dense colonies producing honeydew. Common in urban plantings.
Asian woolly hackberry aphid — white woolly aphid introduced from Asia. Forms dense colonies on hackberry (Celtis) leaves. Produces copious honeydew.

fm.Color: White waxy coating on hackberry leaves

desc: Six-spotted leafhopper — small green leafhopper with six dark spots on vertex. On oak and other hardwoods. Named for entomologist McAtee.
Cherubic Poplar Petiole-gall Aphid. Aphid. Colonial sap-feeder.
Glaucous manzanita gall aphid — tiny aphid inducing red leaf-edge galls on manzanita. Related to T. coweni. Common in chaparral wherever manzanita grows.

fm.Color: Dark; red leaf-edge galls on manzanita

desc: Spiraea Aphid. Aphid. Colonial sap-feeder.
Abbreviated stink bug — brown shield bug similar to Euschistus but smaller. On various plants. Less common than other stink bugs.
Blue-eyed plant bug — tiny mirid with distinctive blue-tinted eyes. On various native shrubs in chaparral. Attractive under magnification.
Red-collared seed bug — dark seed bug with reddish-orange collar behind head. Feeds on seeds of various plants. Common in weedy areas and under debris.
Mediterranean mint aphid — small green aphid on mint family plants (Lamiaceae). Introduced from Mediterranean. Common on garden herbs.
Western seed bug — small dark rhyparochromid found under debris and in leaf litter. Feeds on seeds. Common but secretive ground-dwelling species.
Obscure leafhopper — small pale leafhopper. Common but overlooked due to small size. On various grasses and herbs.
Vachon's barklouse — small psocid on tree bark feeding on algae, fungi, and lichens. Gregarious — forms groups on bark surfaces. Harmless.
Red-shouldered Stink Bug. Stink bug. Shield-shaped.
Red-shouldered Stink Bug. Stink bug. Shield-shaped.
Potato psyllid — tiny psyllid vectoring zebra chip disease of potato (Candidatus Liberibacter). Major quarantine concern. Also on tomato and pepper.

fm.Warning: Vectors zebra chip disease — quarantine pest

desc: Ringed lace bug — tiny tingid with delicate wing venation. On various plants. Wing pattern resembles fine lacework — beautiful under magnification.
Oak wax scale — small scale insect producing distinctive waxy secretion on oak bark. Native. Part of the diverse sap-feeding insect community on California oaks.
Spotted-winged psyllid — tiny psyllid with dark wing spots. On various plants. Part of California's diverse psyllid fauna.
Woolly-legged assassin bug — slender thread-legged bug with hairy legs. Remarkable — enters spider webs to steal prey or eat the spider itself. Kleptoparasite.

fm.Behavior: Enters spider webs — steals prey or eats spider

desc: Island mallow psyllid — tiny psyllid on tree mallow (Malva). Introduced from Mediterranean. Can damage ornamental mallows. Waxy secretion on leaves.
Van Duzee's treehopper — small brown treehopper with elongated pronotal horn. On oak twigs. Part of diverse treehopper community on California oaks.

fm.Color: Brown; pronotal horn; on oak twigs

desc: Thread bug — extremely slender assassin bug. Predatory on small insects in spider webs and bark crevices. So thin it's nearly invisible in profile.
Ice plant scale — soft scale heavily infesting ice plant (Carpobrotus). Produces white cottony egg sacs. Can damage ornamental and invasive ice plant groundcover.

fm.Color: Brown with white cottony egg sac; on ice plant

desc: Wright's seed bug — small dark lygaeid found in leaf litter and under debris. Seed-feeding. Named for entomologist Wright. Part of diverse ground-dwelling bug community.
Arizona thread-legged bug — extremely slender stick-like assassin bug. Thread-thin legs and body. Predator in vegetation. Among the most bizarre-looking LA County insects.
Nigra scale — dark soft scale on ornamental plants and citrus. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold. Managed by parasitoid wasps and ladybugs.
Hemispherical scale — smooth dark dome-shaped soft scale on many ornamental plants. Produces honeydew. Named for occurrence on coffee plants. Pantropical.
Delicate planthopper — tiny delphacid planthopper on grasses and sedges. One of many small planthoppers in grassland and marsh habitats.
Eucalyptus shoot psyllid — tiny psyllid on eucalyptus growing tips. Introduced from Australia. Produces white lerp (waxy cover). Damages new growth.
Bristly psyllid — small psyllid on various native shrubs. Part of the diverse native psyllid fauna of California chaparral.
Curtain fig psyllid — tiny psyllid on Ficus species. Introduced from Asia. Causes leaf curling and sooty mold on ornamental figs.
Van Duzee's plant bug — small green mirid on various plants. Named for pioneer West Coast hemipterist E.P. Van Duzee.
Elder Aphid. Aphid. Colonial sap-feeder.
Pine needle aphid — elongated gray-green aphid feeding singly (not colonial) on pine needles. Unusual among aphids in being solitary. Waxy coating.

fm.Color: Gray-green with waxy bloom; solitary on pine needles

desc: Olive psyllid — tiny psyllid on olive trees producing white waxy secretions on shoots. Can reduce fruit set. Introduced from Mediterranean. Pest of ornamental and production olives.

fm.Color: Green; white wax on olive shoots

desc: Lesser lawn leafhopper — tiny green leafhopper on turf grass. Can transmit plant diseases. Very common in irrigated lawns.

fm.Color: Pale green; tiny; on lawn grass

desc: Strauch's barklouse — small psocid on tree bark. Feeds on microflora (algae, fungi). Gregarious. One of several common bark-dwelling psocids in LA.
Cowpea Aphid. Aphid. Colonial sap-feeder.
Hedge nettle stink bug — dark stink bug with orange-red shoulder markings. On hedge nettle (Stachys) and other Lamiaceae. Native. Attractive pattern.
Ringed-legged assassin bug — dark assassin bug with distinctively banded legs. Predatory on other insects in leaf litter and under bark.
Notable water strider — large water strider skating on pond and stream surfaces. Larger than common Gerris. Detects prey vibrations on water film.

fm.Color: Dark brown; skates on water surface

desc: Western plant bug — small predatory mirid. Important natural enemy of whiteflies and spider mites on cotton and vegetables. Beneficial.
Horn's shore bug — tiny semi-aquatic bug of stream margins and wet rocks. Predatory on small arthropods. One of the smallest predatory true bugs.

fm.Color: Dark; on wet rocks near water

desc: Beeblossom Aphid. Aphid. Colonial sap-feeder.
Reduced leafhopper — small greenish leafhopper on various shrubs. Part of the diverse leafhopper fauna of California chaparral.
Smoky poplar aphid — dark aphid on poplar and cottonwood leaves. Colonial. Tends to be tended by ants for honeydew.
Chenopodia plant bug — tiny mirid associated with goosefoot family plants. Recent arrival to California. On Chenopodium and related weeds.
Sand bug — small flat dark seed bug living in sandy soil. Feeds on fallen seeds. Found under debris in dry sandy habitats. Camouflaged against sand.
Reflex scentless plant bug — small green rhopalid. On various weedy plants. Part of the scentless plant bug family — lacks the stink glands of true stink bugs.
Oblique shield bug — small scutellerid with shield covering entire abdomen. On various plants. Metallic-tinted. Part of the shield bug family.
Hooked plant bug — small mirid with hooked clasper structures in males. On various plants in chaparral and gardens.
Red-veined mirid — green plant bug with reddish wing veins. On various plants. Can damage cotton and vegetable crops.
Bead-like cottonwood gall aphid — induces distinctive bead-shaped galls on cottonwood leaf petioles. Complex life cycle alternating between cottonwood and roots of other plants.

fm.Color: In bead-shaped galls on cottonwood petioles

desc: Wounded flatid — small green planthopper. Wedge-shaped at rest. On various ornamental and wild plants. Produces white waxy secretion.
Broad-headed cicada — small cicada with proportionally wide head. Males produce clicking (not buzzing) song. In chaparral and scrub. Hard to locate by sound.
Minute psyllid — tiny psyllid on various plants. Part of the diverse Bactericera genus which includes several important crop psyllids.
Majestic leafhopper — medium brown leafhopper with pale markings. On various trees and shrubs in woodland.
Marbled shield bug — small scutellerid with marbled pattern. Shield covers entire abdomen and wings. On various plants. Attractive patterning.
Prickly pear cochineal — scale insect on Opuntia cactus. Produces carmine red dye. White cottony clusters on cactus pads. Used commercially for natural red dye for centuries.

fm.Color: White cottony masses on cactus; red when crushed
fm.Note: Source of natural carmine red dye

desc: Tiger scentless plant bug — small rhopalid with tiger-like striped pattern. On various plants. Mediterranean origin. Expanding in SoCal.

fm.Color: Brown with dark stripes — tiger pattern

desc: Corymbia psyllid — tiny psyllid on Corymbia (formerly Eucalyptus) species. Introduced from Australia. Part of the guild of eucalyptus-associated psyllids in LA.
Black plant bug — small dark mirid. On various native and ornamental plants. Part of the diverse mirid fauna of California.
Mexican leafhopper — small elongated leafhopper on grasses. Very common in grassland and agricultural areas.
Tuliptree scale — large conspicuous soft scale on tulip tree, magnolia, and linden. Up to ½ inch — one of the largest scale insects. Produces copious honeydew.
Unadorned cactus bug — leaf-footed bug on prickly pear cactus fruit. Plain brown without the ornate tibial expansions of some coreids. Feeds on cactus juice.
Short psyllid — tiny psyllid on various shrubs. Part of the diverse Cacopsylla genus which includes many plant-specific species.
Hollyleaf cherry whitefly — tiny whitefly specific to hollyleaf cherry (Prunus ilicifolia). Native. Part of the specialized insect fauna on this California native shrub.
Gray lawn leafhopper — tiny gray leafhopper very common in turf grass. Can vector phytoplasmas. Often extremely abundant in lawns.

fm.Color: Gray; very tiny; in lawn grass

desc: Scaled leafhopper — small brown leafhopper on oaks and other trees. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community.
Turnip aphid — gray-green aphid on brassica crops. Dense colonies on turnip, radish, and mustard. Important pest. Waxy coating gives dusty appearance.

fm.Color: Gray-green with waxy bloom; on brassicas

desc: Van Duzee's cicada — small clicking cicada of oak woodland. Males produce series of clicks rather than continuous buzz. Named for hemipterist Van Duzee.
Beautiful-winged psocid — small barklouse with attractively patterned wings. On tree bark feeding on algae and lichen. Gregarious.
Tamarix leafhopper — small leafhopper introduced with salt cedar (Tamarix). Host-specific — only on tamarix. May serve as inadvertent biocontrol of invasive salt cedar.
Rusty plum aphid — reddish-brown aphid alternating between plum (winter) and grasses (summer). Complex life cycle. Can damage plum tree leaves.
Giant reed aphid — large dark aphid on giant reed (Arundo donax). One of the larger aphid species. Host-specific on Arundo. Dense colonies on stems.

fm.Color: Dark brown; on giant reed stems

desc: Melaleuca psyllid — tiny psyllid on melaleuca/paperbark trees. Introduced from Australia with its host tree. Produces white waxy secretion.
cotton lace bug. Lace bug. Intricate wing pattern. On leaf undersides.
Psyllid. Tiny plant sap feeder. Many form galls.
Essig's woolly oak aphid — white woolly aphid on coast live oak undersides. Named for pioneering CA entomologist E.O. Essig. Produces white waxy wool.

fm.Color: White woolly; on oak leaf undersides

desc: Plum-thistle aphid — green-brown aphid alternating between plum trees (winter host) and thistles (summer host). Classic host-alternating life cycle. Can damage both hosts.
California dictyopharid — small elongated planthopper with pointed head. On grasses in coastal and foothill habitats. Distinctive long narrow profile.
Shiny seed bug — small dark shiny seed bug under debris. Feeds on fallen seeds. Common but secretive. Ground-dwelling.
Chinese Wax Scale. Soft scale. Produces honeydew. On plant stems.
Sycamore scale — tiny scale insect specific to sycamore trees. Lives in bark crevices. Can cause branch dieback in heavy infestations. Native.
Giant willow aphid — one of the largest aphids in the world (up to 6mm). Dark brown with prominent dorsal tubercle. Colonial on willow bark. Impressive for an aphid.

fm.Color: Dark brown; prominent back bump; on willow bark

desc: Bird Cherry-oat Aphid. Aphid. Colonial sap-feeder. Important plant pest.
Onion aphid — pale green aphid specializing on onion family plants (Allium). Can damage garlic, leeks, and ornamental alliums. Dense colonies on leaves.
Western dicyphus — small green-black predatory mirid. Important biocontrol agent — feeds on whiteflies, spider mites, and moth eggs in greenhouses.
Coquillett's plant bug — small green mirid. Named for pioneering dipterist D.W. Coquillett. On various native plants in chaparral.
Smooth seed bug — small dark lygaeid found under debris. Feeds on seeds. Secretive ground-dweller of dry habitats.
Beet leafhopper — tiny pale leafhopper. Major vector of curly top virus devastating beets, tomatoes, and many crops. One of the most important pest leafhoppers in western agriculture.

fm.Disease: Vectors curly top virus — major crop disease

desc: Ficus whitefly — tiny whitefly specific to ficus trees. Introduced from Asia. Can cause severe defoliation of ornamental ficus. Very common in urban LA.
Ficus leaf-rolling psyllid. Triozid psyllid. Plant gall former.
Confused pirate bug — tiny predatory anthocorid feeding on thrips in leaf curl galls. Introduced for biocontrol. Important natural enemy of pest thrips.
Bald planthopper — small cixiid planthopper. Larvae feed on plant roots underground. Adults hop among vegetation. Part of the cryptic root-feeding insect community.
Bay sucker — tiny psyllid forming pit galls on bay laurel (Laurus) leaves. Introduced from Europe. Common on ornamental bay laurel in LA gardens.

fm.Color: Tiny; pit galls on bay laurel leaves

desc: Manzanita cicada — medium dark cicada singing from manzanita and other chaparral shrubs. Opaque wings. Buzzing song in summer heat. Foothill species.

fm.Color: Dark; opaque wings; sings from manzanita

desc: Robust ceanothus bug — green mirid plant bug associated with ceanothus (California lilac). Host-specific native species.
Black stink bug — small dark stink bug with punctured surface. On various plants. Can emit defensive stink. Less common than green stink bugs.
Rose Gum Psyllid. Psyllid. Tiny plant sap feeder. Many form galls.
Kirby's backswimmer — aquatic bug swimming upside-down. Predatory — catches aquatic insects and tadpoles. Can deliver painful bite. In ponds and pools.
Juniper Stink Bug. Stink bug. Shield-shaped. Defensive odor when disturbed.
Blood-colored Milkweed Bug. Seed bug. Feeds on seeds and plant tissue.
Nettle ground bug — small brown bug on nettles and related plants. Introduced from Europe. Common in moist disturbed habitats.
Small Willow Aphid. Aphid. Colonial sap-feeder. Important plant pest.
Hibiscus scentless plant bug — pale rhopalid associated with hibiscus and related mallows. Feeds on developing seeds. Common in ornamental plantings.

fm.Color: Pale brown; on hibiscus seed pods

desc: Bear assassin bug — dark robust assassin bug. Predatory ambush hunter in leaf litter. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
tobira psyllid. Psyllid. Tiny plant sap feeder. Many form galls.
Leafhopper. Plant sap feeder. Jumps when disturbed.
Avocado lace bug — tiny lace-winged bug on avocado leaf undersides causing brown stippling. Introduced from tropical Americas. Pest of backyard avocados.
Volxem's coreid — medium brown leaf-footed bug. Introduced from Mediterranean. On various plants. Expanding in SoCal.
Human louse — ectoparasitic insect living on human body/clothing. Blood-feeder. Can vector typhus and relapsing fever. Medically significant.
Bordering barklouse — small psocid on tree bark feeding on algae and lichens. Gregarious. Harmless decomposer.
Speckled barklouse — small psocid on tree bark. Feeds on microflora. One of many bark-dwelling psocids that form the overlooked bark micro-ecosystem.
Two-lined burrowing bug — small dark burrower bug living in soil around plant roots. Shiny. Two pale lines on pronotum. Feeds on root sap.

fm.Color: Dark shiny; two pale pronotal lines; in soil

desc: Oblique-banded seed bug — small dark seed bug with oblique pale wing band. Under debris feeding on seeds. Common in weedy areas.

fm.Color: Dark with oblique pale wing band

desc: Narrow scentless plant bug — slender pale rhopalid on various plants. Unlike stink bugs, lacks strong defensive odor. On weedy and native plants.
Small shield bug — tiny scutellerid with shield covering entire abdomen. On grasses. One of the smallest shield bugs. Easily overlooked.

fm.Color: Brown; shield covers entire back; very small

desc: Corn Leaf Aphid. Aphid. Colonial sap-feeder. Important plant pest.
Tristania Psyllid. Psyllid. Tiny plant sap feeder. Many form galls.
California Lilac Aphid. Aphid. Colonial sap-feeder. Important plant pest.
Leafhopper. Plant sap feeder. Jumps when disturbed.
Impressed-neck seed bug — small dark seed bug with pitted pronotum. Under debris in dry habitats. Feeds on fallen seeds.
Plant bug. Sap feeder on various plants.
Crapemyrtle aphid — tiny yellow aphid specific to crapemyrtle trees. Produces copious honeydew causing sooty mold. Introduced from Asia. Very common.

fm.Color: Yellow; on crapemyrtle; causes sooty mold

desc: Psyllid. Tiny plant sap feeder. Many form galls.
Mistletoe psyllid — tiny psyllid forming galls on mistletoe (Phoradendron). Host-specific. Part of the diverse psyllid fauna associated with California native plants.
Pasadena treehopper — small brown treehopper named for Pasadena, CA. On oak and other trees. Horn-shaped pronotum. Part of diverse treehopper fauna.

fm.Color: Brown; horn-shaped pronotum; named for Pasadena

desc: Ramona plant bug — small green mirid on native vegetation. Named for Ramona, CA. On various chaparral shrubs.
Somber cicada — dark cicada of foothill chaparral. Males produce buzzing song from shrubs. Less common than other Okanagana species.
Ragged leafhopper — small brown leafhopper with worn/ragged-looking markings. On various shrubs.
Handsome cicada — medium cicada with clear wings. Males sing loudly from shrubs in chaparral. As the name suggests, one of the more attractive cicadas.
Rose-colored plant bug — small reddish mirid on various plants. Feeds on plant sap. Part of the enormous and species-rich plant bug genus Phytocoris.
Short-stigma psyllid — tiny psyllid on native shrubs in chaparral. Part of the diverse California native psyllid fauna.
Black-green plant bug — small dark mirid with greenish tinge. On native plants. Part of the diverse mirid fauna of California.
Red-veined cicada — cicada with distinctive reddish wing veins. Males sing from shrubs in foothill habitats. Part of California's endemic cicada fauna.
Brown Stink Bug. Stink bug. Shield-shaped. Defensive odor when disturbed.
Potato leafhopper — tiny green wedge-shaped leafhopper. Major pest causing 'hopperburn' on potatoes, beans, and alfalfa. Migrant from eastern US.
Modest plant bug — small green mirid on various plants. Part of the diverse but poorly known native plant bug fauna.
Southern Small Milkweed Bug. Seed bug. Feeds on seeds and plant tissue.
Minor stink bug — small pale elongated stink bug on grasses. More slender than typical pentatomids. Feeds on grass seeds.

fm.Color: Pale; elongated for a stink bug; on grasses

desc: Coquillett's seed bug — small dark seed bug under debris. Named for pioneering dipterist D.W. Coquillett. Ground-dwelling.
Plant bug. Sap feeder on various plants.
California laurel aphid — woolly aphid producing white waxy tufts on California bay laurel (Umbellularia) leaves. Native species on a native tree.

fm.Color: White woolly tufts on bay laurel

desc: Rose grain aphid — green aphid alternating between roses (winter) and cereals (summer). Important crop pest. Can transmit barley yellow dwarf virus.
Ash whitefly — tiny whitefly on ash, pomegranate, and other trees. Introduced from Mediterranean. Managed effectively by introduced parasitoid Encarsia inaron.
Prickly lettuce aphid — pink-green aphid on lettuce and related Asteraceae. Can damage lettuce crops. Produces honeydew.
Tipped plant bug — small dark mirid. Predatory — feeds on mites and small insects. Beneficial in gardens.
Elegant plant bug — small attractively marked mirid. On native vegetation in chaparral.
Mountain mahogany psyllid — tiny psyllid specific to mountain mahogany (Cercocarpus). Native species on a native host. Chaparral habitat.
Blackberry leafhopper — small green-yellow leafhopper on blackberry and raspberry. Can cause white stippling on leaves. Common in gardens.
Pale desert plant bug — tiny pale mirid of desert and arid scrub. On various plants including saltbush and sage.
Eroded leafhopper — small pale leafhopper on various trees. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community.
California oak aphid — tiny aphid on coast live oak (Q. agrifolia) leaves. Very common — almost every live oak has them. Native.

fm.Color: Pale; on oak leaf undersides; very common

desc: Spiny leafhopper — small leafhopper with spiny vertex. On oaks and other trees.
Extended seed bug — small pale seed bug. Feeds on seeds of various plants. Under debris in dry habitats.
Chenopod psyllid — tiny psyllid on goosefoot family plants (Chenopodium). Forms galls on leaves. On weedy plants.
Reticulate-winged booklouse — tiny wingless psocid in damp indoor spaces. Feeds on mold and starchy materials. Can infest stored foods and old books.
Kellogg's leafhopper — small leafhopper on oaks. Named for entomologist Vernon Kellogg. Part of the oak canopy insect community.
Purple scale — elongated curved armored scale on citrus. Major citrus pest. Mussel-shaped cover distinguishes from round California red scale. Biocontrol by parasitoids.
prickly pear scale. Armored scale. Circular waxy cover. On bark and leaves.
Oleander scale. Armored scale. Circular waxy cover. On bark and leaves.
Splendid stink bug — colorful predatory stink bug. Feeds on beetle larvae — important predator of Colorado potato beetle. Beneficial.
Bailey's acacia psyllid — tiny psyllid on Acacia baileyana (Cootamundra wattle). Introduced from Australia with its host.
Common alder aphid — tiny aphid on alder leaves. Introduced from Europe. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold.
Strawberry tree aphid — green aphid on arbutus (strawberry tree). Introduced from Mediterranean. On ornamental plantings.
American cochineal — scale insect producing carmine red dye. On prickly pear cactus. Historical source of red pigment for textiles and food coloring.

fm.Color: White waxy coating; crushed = brilliant red dye
fm.Note: Source of carmine red dye — historic

desc: Spiny pirate bug — tiny predatory anthocorid with spiny body. Feeds on thrips and mites. Beneficial predator in gardens.
Intermediate plant bug — small mirid on various plants. Part of the diverse mirid fauna.
Mesquite psyllid — tiny psyllid on mesquite trees. Native. Can cause leaf distortion. Part of desert plant-insect community.
Slender gyponana — medium leafhopper with elongated body. On various trees. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community.
California scale-eating ladybug — tiny coccinellid that preys on scale insects. Classified in both Coccinellidae and historically associated with Hemiptera due to its prey.
Dear plant bug — small mirid on various plants.
Nesting whitefly — tiny whitefly building nest-like waxy structures on leaf undersides. Unusual nesting behavior among whiteflies. On citrus and ornamentals.
Twin-spotted leafhopper — small leafhopper with paired dark spots. On various plants. Vectors phytoplasmas.
False indigo plant bug — small mirid on false indigo (Amorpha). Host-specific. Native bug on a native plant.
Buckwheat plant bug — small mirid associated with buckwheat (Eriogonum). Host-specific. Important component of buckwheat insect community.
Worm-marked leafhopper — small leafhopper with vermiculate (worm-like) markings. On oaks.
Magnificent cicada — medium-large cicada. Males produce loud buzzing song. One of the more impressive California cicadas.
Variegated leafhopper — small mottled leafhopper. Vectors phytoplasma diseases of various crops and ornamentals.
Wounded cicada — medium cicada with reddish wing veins suggesting 'wounds.' Males sing loudly from shrubs. Foothill chaparral.
Brilliant plant bug — small shiny predatory mirid. Important predator of aphids and mites. Beneficial in gardens.
Angular plant bug — small green mirid on willows and other trees. Native. Common in riparian habitats.
Shoshone shield bug — large scutellerid with shield covering entire back. On grasses. Impressive full-body shield shape.
Similar wing-clapper — small cicada that produces clicking sounds by wing-clapping rather than typical cicada buzzing. Unusual sound production.

fm.Note: Clicks wings instead of buzzing — unusual

desc: Frontal delphacid — tiny planthopper on grasses. Part of the diverse grass-feeding planthopper community.
Spiny psyllid — tiny psyllid on native shrubs. Part of the diverse California psyllid fauna.
Hoffman's backswimmer — aquatic bug swimming upside-down. Predatory. In ponds and pools. Can bite if handled.
Ceanothus plant bug — green mirid specific to ceanothus (California lilac). Native bug on a native shrub. Part of diverse ceanothus insect community.
Gill's whitefly — tiny whitefly on native trees. Part of the diverse California whitefly fauna.
Briggs' barklouse — small psocid on tree bark. Feeds on algae and lichens. Gregarious.
Single-spotted barklouse — small psocid with single dark wing spot. On tree bark. Feeds on microflora.
Transparent barklouse — small psocid with clear/hyaline wings. On tree bark. Feeds on bark microflora.
Spined assassin bug — medium assassin bug with crown-like spines on pronotum. Important predator of pest caterpillars and beetles. Can bite if handled.
Long-necked seed bug — distinctive seed bug with extremely elongated head. Fast runner. Under debris. Unique long head is diagnostic.
Manzanita ambush bug — tiny ambush bug hiding in manzanita flowers. Raptorial forelegs grab prey much larger than itself.
Common damsel bug — medium predatory bug. Important biocontrol agent — catches aphids, caterpillars, and other pests. Slender raptorial forelegs.
Pear sucker — tiny psyllid on pear trees. Major pest causing pear decline disease. Honeydew production attracts sooty mold. Important orchard pest.
Japanese maple leafhopper — small pale leafhopper introduced from Asia. On Japanese maples and other ornamentals. Can cause stippling.
Mulsant's water treader — tiny bug that walks on water surface film. Semi-aquatic. Predatory on mosquito larvae and small insects trapped in surface tension.
Depicted seed bug — small dark seed bug with pale markings. Under debris.
Foxglove aphid — pale green aphid with dark knee spots (diagnostic). On potatoes, lettuce, foxglove, and many plants. Vectors plant viruses.
Elegant leafhopper — small leafhopper with refined markings. On various shrubs in chaparral.
Reddish plant bug — small mirid with reddish tinge. On various plants.
Leafcurl plum aphid — green aphid causing severe leaf curling on plum trees. Migrates to composites in summer. Can distort plum shoots.
Hakea psyllid — tiny psyllid on hakea trees. Introduced from Australia. On ornamental hakea plantings.
Black olive scale — dark hemispherical scale with H-shaped ridge on back. On olive, citrus, and ornamentals. Important olive pest. H-mark diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark; H-shaped ridge on back — diagnostic

desc: Yucca plant bug — small dark mirid on yucca plants. Can cause stippling damage to yucca leaves. Native.
Yucca sharpshooter — green sharpshooter associated with yucca. Part of the diverse sharpshooter fauna. Can vector Xylella.
Beautiful-ruled plant bug — tiny predatory mirid. Feeds on whiteflies and mites. Beneficial.
Yerba santa lace bug — tiny lace bug on yerba santa (Eriodictyon). Host-specific native species.
Wrinkle-winged leafhopper — small dark leafhopper with rugose (wrinkled) wing surface. Nocturnal.
Sand-dwelling seed bug — small dark seed bug introduced from Europe. Under debris in sandy habitats. Expanding in SoCal.
Mulberry whitefly — tiny whitefly on mulberry trees. Can cause sooty mold from honeydew production. On ornamental mulberries.
Manzanita whitefly — tiny whitefly on manzanita (Arctostaphylos) leaf undersides. Native species on a native host. Produces waxy filaments.
Leafcurl ash aphid — woolly aphid causing severe leaf curling on ash trees. White waxy secretion. Can distort and drop ash leaves.
California triozid — tiny psyllid on native plants. Part of the diverse California psyllid fauna.
Flannelbush psyllid — tiny psyllid on flannelbush (Fremontodendron). Native specialist on a native shrub. Forms galls on leaves.
Gelatinous whitefly — tiny whitefly producing gelatinous waxy secretion on leaves. On various trees.
Bougainvillea mealybug — white waxy mealybug on bougainvillea and other ornamentals. Can cause leaf distortion and drop. Honeydew attracts sooty mold.
Stuffed plant bug — small mirid. On native vegetation.
Spiny lace bug — tiny lace bug with pronounced spines. On various shrubs.
Essig's ceanothus bug — mirid on ceanothus. Named for pioneer CA entomologist E.O. Essig.
Creosote lac scale — scale insect producing lac (resin) on creosote bush stems. Native. Creates conspicuous dark encrustations on creosote branches.
Dwarf leafhopper — very small leafhopper. Among the smaller leafhoppers. On various plants.
Golden-streaked leafhopper — small leafhopper with golden streaks. On birch and alder. Attractive markings.
Red-written plant bug — mirid with reddish markings. On various plants.
Minute ceanothus bug — very small mirid on ceanothus. Smallest of the ceanothus-associated plant bugs.
Ringed booklouse — tiny wingless psocid. Indoors in damp conditions. Feeds on mold.
Black bean aphid — dark aphid forming dense colonies on broad beans, chard, and ornamentals. Major garden pest. Complex host-alternating life cycle.
Barbara's barklouse — small psocid on bark. Feeds on microflora.
Robust barklouse — small psocid on bark. Larger than most psocids.
Related rough stink bug — large bark-colored stink bug. Feeds on tree fruits. Bark-cryptic when resting on tree trunks. Rough textured.
Western bat bug — ectoparasite of bats related to bed bugs. In bat roosts. Can temporarily bite humans when bats are excluded from buildings.
Waterlily aphid — dark aphid on waterlilies and aquatic plants. One of few truly aquatic aphids. Can damage ornamental pond plants.

fm.Color: Dark; on waterlilies and aquatic plants

desc: Oak lace bug — tiny bug with intricate lace wing pattern on oak leaf undersides. Causes stippling damage. Beautiful under magnification.

fm.Color: White lace pattern; on oak leaves

desc: Dorsal scentless plant bug — small pale rhopalid. On various weedy plants.
Pale-edged stink bug — medium green-brown stink bug with pale edges. On various plants.
Half-banded seed bug — small dark seed bug with partial pale band. Under bark and debris.
Bald stink bug — small green stink bug. On various plants. Feeds on fruit and developing seeds.
Antigua plant bug — small dark mirid. Introduced from Central America. On various plants.
American blue bug — small metallic blue predatory stink bug. Feeds on flea beetles and other chrysomelids. Beneficial. One of the most colorful pentatomids.

fm.Color: Metallic blue; predatory on flea beetles

desc: Grain aphid — green aphid similar to S. avenae. On cereals and grasses. Important crop pest.
Coreopsis aphid — small dark aphid on coreopsis and related composites. Can build dense colonies on flower stems.
Brown soft scale — soft scale insect on citrus, ficus, and many ornamentals. Produces copious honeydew. Major nursery pest.
Green coffee scale — pale green soft scale on coffee, citrus, and ornamentals. Tropical origin. Produces honeydew.
Green shield scale — soft scale producing white cottony egg sac. On guava, citrus, and many ornamentals. Tropical pest established in SoCal.
Dogwood-milkweed aphid — green aphid alternating between dogwood and milkweed. Complex seasonal host-switching life cycle.
Pendulous plant bug — small mirid. On native vegetation.
Delicate plant bug — very small fragile mirid. On native vegetation in chaparral.
Branched plant bug — small mirid on various trees and shrubs. Part of the species-rich Phytocoris genus.
Sowthistle aphid — green aphid alternating between currant (winter) and sowthistle (summer). Vectors lettuce necrotic yellows virus.
European fruit scale — brown convex scale on many fruit and ornamental trees. Introduced from Europe. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold.
Black-spotted bamboo aphid — tiny pale aphid with dark spots on bamboo. Introduced from Asia. Can coat bamboo leaves with honeydew.

fm.Color: Pale with dark spots; on bamboo

desc: Spotted alfalfa aphid — tiny yellow aphid with dark dorsal spots. Major pest of alfalfa — can devastate stands. Injects toxic saliva.
Paddock's evening bug — small mirid. Named for collector Paddock.
California plant bug — small mirid endemic to California. On native shrubs in chaparral.
Behrens' treehopper — small treehopper on oaks. Named for California naturalist James Behrens.
Swallow nest bug — blood-feeding bug living in cliff swallow nests. Related to bed bugs. Can enter homes when swallows nest on buildings.
White sage aphid — aphid on white sage (Artemisia ludoviciana) and related sagebrush. Native aphid on native plants.
California willow aphid — green aphid on willows. Native species. Part of the diverse California aphid fauna.
Blood-red psyllid — tiny psyllid with reddish coloring. On native shrubs.
English grain aphid — green aphid on wheat, barley, and other cereals. Major crop pest. Vectors barley yellow dwarf virus.
Lined spittlebug — small green froghopper. Nymphs produce frothy spittle masses on plant stems. Adults jump powerfully.
Protean shield bug — small shield bug. Variable coloring (hence 'proteus'). On grasses.
Beautiful shore bug — small dark saldid found at water margins. Predatory. Jumps and flies short distances when disturbed.
Laburnum aphid — aphid on broom and related legumes. Introduced from Europe. On ornamental broom and genista.
Desert bee assassin — large red-and-black assassin bug of desert habitats. Ambushes pollinators on flowers. Related to California bee assassin.
Ash-scrub plant bug — small mirid on sagebrush and related plants.
Black-banded stink bug — small stink bug with black and reddish-orange banding. On various plants. Related to C. conspicillaris.
Red date scale — tiny red-colored scale insect on date palm fronds. Can damage date production. Named for entomologist C.L. Marlatt.
Dorsal plant bug — small mirid with dorsal markings. On various plants.
Guava aphid — dark aphid with long cornicles on guava and related plants. Introduced from Asia. On ornamental Myrtaceae.
Shining green tarragon aphid — green glossy aphid on Artemisia (sagebrush/tarragon). Unusually shiny for an aphid.
Nichol's treehopper — small dark treehopper. Named for its dark 'tuxedo' coloring. On various plants.
Shield-faced planthopper — small pale green acanaloniid. Broad flat body resembles a leaf fragment. On various trees and shrubs.
Caldwell's delphacid — tiny planthopper on grasses. Part of the diverse but poorly known planthopper fauna.
Boheman's seed bug — small dark seed bug. Under debris. Feeds on fallen seeds.
Green triozid — tiny green psyllid. On various plants.
Intermediate plant bug — small green mirid on various plants.
Agile plant bug — small active mirid. Runs quickly on foliage. On various plants.
Romantic leafhopper — small pale leafhopper. On various plants. One of the more poetically named insects.
Zigzag treehopper — tiny treehopper with zigzag horn on pronotum. On oaks. Part of the diverse treehopper fauna.
Oman's seed bug — small dark seed bug named for entomologist P.W. Oman. Under debris.
Vendor's plant bug — small mirid. Part of the enormous and diverse plant bug family.
Atriplex ensign scale — scale insect with white waxy plates arranged like flags/ensigns. On saltbush (Atriplex). Native.
Tubercle-legged burrowing bug — small dark cydnid with bumpy legs. Lives in soil around plant roots. Feeds on root sap.
Black seed bug — small black seed bug. Under debris in dry habitats. Feeds on seeds.
Marked plant bug — small mirid with distinctive dark marks. On native vegetation.
Red-veined scentless plant bug — pale rhopalid with reddish wing veins. On various plants.
European elm scale — cottony scale on elms producing white waxy secretion. Introduced from Europe. Can weaken elm trees.

fm.Color: Brown with white waxy fringe; on elms

desc: Coquillett's barklouse — small psocid. Named for dipterist D.W. Coquillett.
Manzanita scale — armored scale insect on manzanita bark. White waxy cover. Native species on a native host.

fm.Color: White waxy cover on manzanita bark
